title: National Human Trafficking Database Act
stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.
plain_summary: This bill creates a national online database to track human trafficking cases across the United States. The Office for Victims of Crime will give money to state law enforcement agencies to collect information about trafficking cases, court cases, and groups that help victims. All this information will be put together in one place online and updated every year. The database will help people understand how big the human trafficking problem is while keeping victims' personal information private and safe.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.
subjects: Social work, volunteer service, charitable organizations

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the National Human Trafficking Database Act.

sections.title: Section 2  National Human Trafficking Database
sections.text: This section creates a new part in an existing crime control law. The new part sets up a national database to track human trafficking information. Human trafficking means forcing people to work or selling them for sex. Labor trafficking is when people are forced to work against their will. Sex trafficking is when people are forced into prostitution or other sexual activities for money. An anti-human trafficking organization is a group whose main job is to fight human trafficking by helping people at risk, raising awareness, and providing services to survivors. A covered state agency means either a state police investigation unit or another state agency chosen by the state to coordinate the response to human trafficking. This could be a public health agency or another agency the state picks. The Director of the Office means the person who runs the Office for Victims of Crime. Primary service means the main thing an anti-human trafficking organization does, like preventing trafficking, finding survivors, rescuing people, or helping survivors get out of trafficking situations. The Office for Victims of Crime must give grants to covered state agencies to collect and report human trafficking data. State agencies must apply for these grants and promise to start collecting data within one hundred eighty days of getting the money. They can only use the grant money to collect and report this specific data. Within one hundred eighty days of this law passing, the Director must create guidelines for how to use the grant money. Each state agency that gets a grant must report specific data to the Director within one year of this law passing. This includes the number of prosecutions, arrests, and convictions for human trafficking in each county. They must also report anonymous data from trafficking hotlines, children's services agencies, and other government offices. They need to list crimes that often happen alongside trafficking, like prostitution, drug dealing, sexual assault, and gang violence. They must count how many trafficking survivors got help from nonprofit organizations. They also need to name each anti-human trafficking organization in each county and what their main service is. For state-level prosecutions, they must sort the data by sex, race, citizenship, primary language, and previous convictions. If any of this information is not available, they must explain why. Within eighteen months of this law passing, the Director must publish a database on the Department of Justice website. This database will show all the data that states reported. It will list the ten counties in each state with the highest and lowest rates of human trafficking. If a state has fewer than twenty counties, it will rank all counties from highest to lowest trafficking rates. The database will also show the number of federal prosecutions, arrests, and convictions in each state. State agencies can work with many federal offices to collect information about anti-human trafficking organizations. These include the office that runs the National Human Trafficking Hotline, the Office for Victims of Crime, the Office on Violence Against Women, and several others. Starting in fiscal year two thousand twenty five, states must report their data within ninety days after each fiscal year ends. The Director must update the database within one hundred eighty days after each fiscal year ends using the new data from states and federal law enforcement. The Director must also report the database contents to Congress within one hundred eighty days after each fiscal year ends. State agencies must protect the privacy and safety of trafficking survivors and their families when collecting and reporting data. The Department of Justice cannot use this data to make funding decisions. Neither the Department of Justice nor state agencies can demand, access, or make public any personal information that could identify trafficking survivors. Congress approves spending fifty million dollars each year from fiscal year two thousand twenty five through two thousand twenty eight for the grants to states. It also approves one million dollars each year for the Director to create, update, and report on the database. This money stays available until it is spent.
